original: Top of the line holographic controls project from the port wall to control the ship’s central ramp. It lies under you, almost seamlessly meshed with the floor itself, unmarred by the bulky pneumatic pistons you would find on other vessels. Arrays of electromagnetic clamps and repulsors lie underfoot instead, ready to drop or raise the heavy metal plating on your command. You can exit here. The Spearhead SS’s helm lies south, toward the nose of the vessel. A security station is just north.

You can see more of the void here than almost anywhere else about this ship, courtesy of the generous dappling of portholes along its neck. Of course these portholes are made of six-inch thick transparent aluminum sheathed in redundant hardlight safety barriers rather than flimsy glass. Inside, things are better lit. Track lighting on the floor and ceiling guarantees ample illumination along the neck’s single, wide corridor.

Blinking lights. A hovering chair. Arrays of physical switches overlaid by holographic indicators and data that mere physical switches could never hope to communicate. This is the kind of cockpit KihaCorp puts in its vessels. From here, you could fly this ship anywhere.

The ship’s communications and security center lies smack dab in its center - between passages to every conceivable corner of the ship. There are even two small lifts for traveling to the upper or lower decks, where any crew would stay - or where additional armaments might be installed.\n\n\t\tYou can summon your crew from this security console.

The Spearhead SS may not have the most spacious cargo bay. It may not have the best cargo bay. It may not even have the best illuminated cargo bay, but it does have a cargo bay, albeit one designed around its sporty body and efficient XFold engines.\n\n\t\tThis is a good place to store spare possessions between missions.

Nestled tightly against the XFold engine’s housing, this small space appears to have been intended to serve as a maintenance bay. Sockets from a half-dozen different standards festoon the walls, floor, and even the ceiling.\n\n\t\tYou may install personal electronic devices here that are too large to carry but too small to fit to the ship.

Nestled snugly amongst additional armored bulkheads, this Captain’s quarters is as comfortable as it is cozy. Fold-away furniture allows the room to be everything from a sensible workspace to a decadent harem chamber. Silver wood trim harvested from some far-off luxury world graces the chromapanel walls with a taste of the organic. There are even heated floors in addition to the standard environmental controls one might expect.

This room lies at the very aft of the vessel, snugly in the center of the ‘X’ of the Xfold engines. Instead of utilizing weaker transparent panels or fallible hardlight projections, KihaCorp chose a remote camera system and a high resolution panel, displaying a razor-sharp view of stars and the four main engine plumes when in flight.

The Port side XFold engine intrudes into the tight corridor, narrowing down to the tiniest passage a sapient could be expected to squeeze through. There is little reason to spend time in this miserable nook - unless you’re in need of in-flight repair or a member of a thermally sensitive species who finds the sweltering rumbles enjoyable.
